title: The curtain that drones don't notice
summary: The Faroe Islands deliver post by bicycle on routes a van cannot use. A drone might replace the bicycle — except for one detail nobody can automate.
body:
On most of the Faroe Islands, you cannot drive a postal van. The roads are too narrow, the villages are too small, or there are no roads at all between the houses. The islands have eighteen inhabited spots in the Atlantic Ocean, north of Scotland and west of Norway, with a total population of about fifty-four thousand people. The postal service has decided, since 1976, that the most reliable way to deliver letters in many of them is by bicycle.

There are sixty-three bicycle postmen and postwomen on the islands. They are called 'postcyklisterne' in the local Faroese language. Each of them covers between forty and seventy kilometres a day, in weather that is rarely calm. The wind on the islands averages thirty-two kilometres an hour. Rain is so common that postmen do not call a rainy day 'rainy.' They call it 'a day.'

I spoke to one of them, Birna Olsen, who has been a bicycle postman since she was twenty-two. She is now forty-one. Her route runs along a coastal road near the village of Saksun. 'The wind is the boss,' she said. 'You learn its moods. You learn when to push and when to walk the bike. You learn that wet hands do not need gloves. They need warmer skin.'

The job is not romantic. Birna's two predecessors retired because of knee injuries. The bike she rides is specially adapted: it has eleven gears, a low frame, and a small waterproof bag that holds about twenty letters. Her shift begins at half past five in the morning. She finishes at half past eleven. In the worst storms, the post does not move at all.

But there is something the bicycle does that a van could not. 'I see the houses,' Birna told me. 'If old Mr Tórleif has not raised his curtain by nine, I knock on the door. In a van you do not see the curtain. You see the road.' Twice in her career, she has called an ambulance from a doorstep, on a normal delivery. Once it mattered.

There is now a slow, polite argument inside the Faroese postal service about whether the bicycle posts should remain. Drones could deliver in many of the same places, faster and without injury risk. The argument against drones is not technological. It is the curtain. A drone does not notice a curtain.

For now, Birna and the others continue. Letters are fewer than they were ten years ago, but the route is the same. In May, the puffins return to the cliffs. In November, the dark begins to win. The wind is still the boss. The curtain is still being watched.

annotations.meaningVi: thiết bị bay không người lái

grammarNotes.title: Relative clauses with 'who', 'which', 'that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mệnh đề quan hệ thêm thông tin về một danh từ. Dùng 'who' cho người, 'which' cho vật, 'that' cho cả hai. Trong bài: 'Birna Olsen, who has been a bicycle postman since she was twenty-two' (thông tin về người). 'a small waterproof bag that holds about twenty letters' (thông tin về vật). 'something the bicycle does that a van could not' (lược bỏ 'which/that' khi nó là tân ngữ). Mệnh đề có dấu phẩy hai bên (không bắt buộc) — bỏ ra vẫn hiểu được; mệnh đề không có dấu phẩy (xác định) — phải có để hiểu danh từ là gì.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Relative clauses add information about a noun. Use 'who' for people, 'which' for things, 'that' for either. The passage uses both kinds: non-defining (with commas) — 'Birna Olsen, who has been a bicycle postman since she was twenty-two' — adds extra info; defining (no commas) — 'a small waterproof bag that holds about twenty letters' — identifies which bag. When the relative pronoun is the object, it can be dropped: 'something the bicycle does that a van could not.'

translationVi:
Trên phần lớn quần đảo Faroe, bạn không thể đi xe tải chở thư. Đường quá hẹp, làng quá nhỏ, hoặc giữa các nhà không có đường. Quần đảo có mười tám điểm có người ở giữa Đại Tây Dương, phía bắc Scotland và phía tây Na Uy, với tổng dân số khoảng năm mươi tư nghìn người. Từ năm 1976, ngành bưu chính quyết định: cách giao thư tin cậy nhất ở nhiều nơi là bằng xe đạp.

Có sáu mươi ba 'postcyklisterne' (người đưa thư bằng xe đạp) trên đảo. Mỗi người đi khoảng bốn mươi đến bảy mươi cây số mỗi ngày, trong thời tiết hiếm khi yên. Gió trung bình ở đây ba mươi hai cây số giờ. Mưa nhiều đến mức người đưa thư không gọi ngày mưa là 'ngày mưa'. Họ gọi nó là 'một ngày'.

Tôi nói chuyện với một người trong số họ, Birna Olsen, người làm nghề này từ năm hai mươi hai tuổi. Giờ chị bốn mươi mốt. Tuyến của chị chạy dọc bờ biển gần làng Saksun. 'Gió là sếp,' chị nói. 'Mình học tính nết của gió. Mình học khi nào đạp, khi nào dắt xe. Mình học rằng tay ướt không cần găng. Tay ướt cần da ấm hơn.'

Công việc không lãng mạn. Hai người làm trước Birna về hưu vì chấn thương đầu gối. Chiếc xe đạp được chỉnh riêng: mười một cấp số, sườn thấp, một túi chống nước nhỏ chứa được khoảng hai mươi lá thư. Ca của chị bắt đầu lúc năm rưỡi sáng. Kết thúc lúc mười một rưỡi. Trong bão lớn nhất, thư không di chuyển.

Nhưng có một thứ xe đạp làm được mà xe tải không. 'Tôi thấy các căn nhà,' Birna kể với tôi. 'Nếu chín giờ ông Tórleif vẫn chưa kéo rèm, tôi gõ cửa. Trên xe tải, anh không thấy rèm. Anh chỉ thấy đường.' Hai lần trong sự nghiệp, chị đã gọi cấp cứu ngay trước cửa nhà, trong một buổi giao thư bình thường. Một lần thực sự quan trọng.

Giờ trong ngành bưu chính Faroe có một tranh luận chậm rãi, lịch sự về việc có nên giữ tuyến xe đạp hay không. Drone có thể giao nhiều nơi tương tự, nhanh hơn, không có nguy cơ chấn thương. Lập luận chống lại drone không phải về công nghệ. Mà về cái rèm. Drone không để ý cái rèm.

Hiện tại, Birna và các đồng nghiệp vẫn tiếp tục. Số thư ít hơn mười năm trước, nhưng tuyến vẫn vậy. Tháng Năm, chim puffin trở về vách đá. Tháng Mười Một, bóng tối bắt đầu thắng. Gió vẫn là sếp. Cái rèm vẫn đang được trông chừng.

questions.prompt: Why can't postal vans drive in most of the Faroe Islands?
options: The roads are too narrow, the villages too small, or there are no roads between houses.
questions.explanation: 'The roads are too narrow, the villages are too small, or there are no roads at all between the houses.'

questions.prompt: Since when has the postal service used bicycles?
questions.explanation: 'The postal service has decided, since 1976, that the most reliable way to deliver letters in many of them is by bicycle.'

questions.prompt: What do the postmen call a rainy day?
questions.explanation: 'Rain is so common that postmen do not call a rainy day "rainy." They call it "a day."'

questions.prompt: How long has Birna Olsen been a bicycle postman?
questions.explanation: Since she was twenty-two; she is now forty-one — about nineteen years.
questions.hint: Subtract her age then from her age now.

questions.prompt: How does Birna describe the wind?
options: 'The boss' — she learns its moods, when to push, when to walk the bike
questions.explanation: 'The wind is the boss. You learn its moods.'

questions.prompt: What happened to Birna's two predecessors?
options: They retired because of knee injuries.
questions.explanation: 'Birna's two predecessors retired because of knee injuries.'

questions.prompt: What is special about her bicycle?
options: Eleven gears, a low frame, and a small waterproof bag for about twenty letters
It is the only red one.

questions.prompt: What can the bicycle postman see that a van driver cannot?
options: The curtain of a house — if it has not been raised by nine, she knocks.
questions.explanation: 'I see the houses. If old Mr Tórleif has not raised his curtain by nine, I knock on the door. In a van you do not see the curtain. You see the road.'
questions.hint: Look at Birna's quote about Mr Tórleif.

questions.prompt: How many times has Birna called an ambulance from a doorstep?
questions.explanation: 'Twice in her career, she has called an ambulance from a doorstep, on a normal delivery. Once it mattered.'
questions.hint: Look at the line right after Birna's quote about the curtain.

questions.prompt: What is the argument against using drones, according to the passage?
options: Drones do not notice a curtain — they cannot replace the human watching that the bicycle round provides.
Drones cannot fly in the wind.
questions.explanation: 'The argument against drones is not technological. It is the curtain. A drone does not notice a curtain.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the writer NOT claiming?
options: Bicycle posts should be kept everywhere in the world, regardless of context.
Some old practices survive because they do an extra job that nobody planned for them to do.
The Faroe Islands have a specific geography that makes bicycles practical.
Drones could in fact deliver many of the same letters.
questions.explanation: The writer makes a specific local argument about the curtain, not a global claim about bicycles everywhere.
questions.hint: Look for the overstated, global claim.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A daily route that looks like just 'delivering letters' can quietly do a second job — watching the community — that no technical replacement is shaped to do.
The Faroe Islands have the worst weather in Europe.
Drones should never be used for mail.
questions.explanation: The bicycle round delivers letters and also watches curtains — the second function is what the drone cannot do.
questions.hint: Think about the 'second job' the bicycle does.

cloze.template:
On most of the Faroe Islands, you cannot drive a postal van. The roads are too narrow, the villages are too small, or there are no roads at all between the houses. The islands have eighteen {{b1}} spots in the Atlantic Ocean, north of Scotland and west of Norway, with a total population of about fifty-four thousand people. The postal service has decided, since {{b2}}, that the most reliable way to deliver letters in many of them is by bicycle.

There are sixty-three bicycle postmen and postwomen on the islands. They are called 'postcyklisterne' in the local Faroese {{b3}}. Each of them covers between forty and seventy kilometres a day, in weather that is rarely calm. The wind on the islands averages thirty-two kilometres an hour. Rain is so common that postmen do not call a rainy day 'rainy.' They call it 'a {{b4}}.'

I spoke to one of them, Birna Olsen, who has been a bicycle postman since she was twenty-two. She is now forty-one. Her route runs along a coastal road near the village of Saksun. 'The wind is the {{b5}},' she said. 'You learn its moods. You learn when to push and when to walk the bike. You learn that wet hands do not need gloves. They need warmer skin.'

The job is not romantic. Birna's two {{b6}} retired because of knee injuries. The bike she rides is specially adapted: it has eleven gears, a low frame, and a small waterproof bag that holds about twenty letters. Her shift begins at half past five in the morning. She finishes at half past {{b7}}. In the worst storms, the post does not move at all.

But there is something the bicycle does that a van could not. 'I see the houses,' Birna told me. 'If old Mr Tórleif has not raised his {{b8}} by nine, I knock on the door. In a van you do not see the curtain. You see the road.' Twice in her career, she has called an {{b9}} from a doorstep, on a normal delivery. Once it mattered.

There is now a slow, polite argument inside the Faroese postal service about whether the bicycle posts should remain. Drones could deliver in many of the same places, faster and without injury risk. The argument against drones is not technological. It is the curtain. A {{b10}} does not notice a curtain.

For now, Birna and the others continue. Letters are fewer than they were ten years ago, but the route is the same. In May, the {{b11}} return to the cliffs. In November, the dark begins to win. The wind is still the boss. The curtain is still being watched.

blanks.explanation: Eighteen inhabited spots — places where people live.
They call a rainy day 'a day.'
Her two predecessors (the postmen before her).
'A drone does not notice a curtain.'
Puffins return to the cliffs in May.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Birna's bicycle round does two jobs: delivering letters and watching the village. Is there a routine in your life or work that quietly does a second job — keeping in touch, noticing changes — that nobody specifically asked for? What would be lost if that routine were 'optimised' away?
